data.title: Returns On Capital Are Showing Encouraging Signs At Montana Aerospace (VTX:AERO)
data.description:
If you're looking for a multi-bagger, there's a few things to keep an eye out for. Firstly, we'll want to see a proven return on capital employed (ROCE) that is increasing, and secondly, an expanding base of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at Montana Aerospace (VTX:AERO) and its trend of ROCE, we really liked what we saw.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

If you haven't worked with ROCE before, it measures the 'return' (pre-tax profit) a company generates from capital employed in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Montana Aerospace:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.076 = €106m ÷ (€1.6b - €212m) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, Montana Aerospace has an ROCE of 7.6%. In absolute terms, that's a low return and it also under-performs the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 12%.

View our latest analysis for Montana Aerospace

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Montana Aerospace compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for Montana Aerospace .

What Can We Tell From Montana Aerospace's ROCE Trend?

We're delighted to see that Montana Aerospace is reaping rewards from its investments and is now generating some pre-tax profits. About five years ago the company was generating losses but things have turned around because it's now earning 7.6% on its capital. Not only that, but the company is utilizing 54% more capital than before, but that's to be expected from a company trying to break into profitability. This can indicate that there's plenty of opportunities to invest capital internally and at ever higher rates, both common traits of a multi-bagger.

On a related note, the company's ratio of current liabilities to total assets has decreased to 13%, which basically reduces it's funding from the likes of short-term creditors or suppliers. So this improvement in ROCE has come from the business' underlying economics, which is great to see.

What We Can Learn From Montana Aerospace's ROCE

Long story short, we're delighted to see that Montana Aerospace's reinvestment activities have paid off and the company is now profitable. And with the stock having performed exceptionally well over the last three years, these patterns are being accounted for by investors. So given the stock has proven it has promising trends, it's worth researching the company further to see if these trends are likely to persist.

Like most companies, Montana Aerospace does come with some risks, and we've found 2 warning signs that you should be aware of.

data.title: Ichiken (TSE:1847) Low P/E And ¥5.2b TTM Earnings Power Test Valuation Narrative
data.description:
Ichiken (TSE:1847) has reported another solid quarter, with Q2 2026 revenue of ¥27.0b and basic EPS of ¥194.29, alongside net income of ¥1,410m. The trailing twelve months to Q2 show revenue of ¥96.6b, basic EPS of ¥719.53 and net income of ¥5,222m. Over the last few reported periods, the company has seen revenue move from ¥26.6b and EPS of ¥218.07 in Q3 2025 to ¥20.8b and EPS of ¥153.69 in Q4 2025, then to ¥24.6b and EPS of ¥173.86 in Q1 2026 and most recently ¥27.0b and EPS of ¥194.29 in Q2 2026. This gives investors a clearer read on how earnings power is tracking against the top line. With the stock at ¥5,710 and profitability metrics holding up, the latest numbers highlight that margins are a key part of the story this quarter.

See our full analysis for Ichiken.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around Ichiken, and where the fresh data might support or challenge those views.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM earnings power at ¥5.2b

  • Over the trailing twelve months, Ichiken generated net income of ¥5.2b on revenue of ¥96.6b, giving you a sense of the profit base that sits behind the recent quarters.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that these earnings are described as high quality, and the last four reported quarters show net income moving between ¥999.5m and ¥1,583m, which suggests profits have been consistently in the billions rather than swinging into losses.
    • Bulls can point to Q2 2026 net income of ¥1,410m and Q1 2026 at ¥1,262m as part of the ¥5.2b trailing figure, which supports the idea of a business that has been steadily profitable over the year.
    • At the same time, EPS has ranged from ¥137.69 in Q1 2025 to ¥218.07 in Q3 2025, so while the absolute level of profit looks solid for the period, the pattern in per share earnings is not a straight line, which is something bullish investors still need to think about.

Curious how this earnings profile fits into a fuller story about the business over time, including risks and opportunities that do not show up in one table of numbers? 📊 Read the full Ichiken Consensus Narrative.

P/E of 7.9x versus 13x peers

  • Ichiken trades on a trailing P/E of 7.9x, compared to a peer average of 13.2x and a Japan construction industry average of 13.7x, so the market is paying a lower multiple for each yen of trailing earnings than it is for many comparable names.
  • Supporters of a more bullish angle see this lower multiple, together with the ¥5.2b of trailing net income, as a sign the shares could be inexpensive relative to both peers and the DCF fair value of ¥5,954.02, while skeptics may question how far you can lean on that when the listed history is still under three years.
    • On one side, a share price of ¥5,710 that sits about 4.1% below the DCF fair value and on a P/E well under the 13x range peers trade at is exactly the kind of setup value focused investors often look for.
    • On the other, critics highlight that the shorter public track record and an unstable dividend record mean there is less evidence about how this earnings and valuation profile holds up across different conditions, which is why the discount might be persisting.

Recent quarters show EPS between ¥153 and ¥218

  • Across the last four reported quarters, basic EPS has moved from ¥218.07 in Q3 2025 to ¥153.69 in Q4 2025, then to ¥173.86 in Q1 2026 and ¥194.29 in Q2 2026, so investors are working with a band of roughly mid 100s to low 200s yen per share in recent periods.
  • For a more cautious, bearish take, that EPS range and the unstable dividend history raise questions about how predictable shareholder returns are, even with steady profits, because both the earnings per share and the income stream to shareholders have shown variability rather than a simple upward trend.
    • Bears argue that this kind of quarter to quarter EPS movement can make it harder to assess what a normal earnings level should be, especially when there is less than three years of public data to compare against.
    • They also point out that while the company has been profitable, the flagged instability in dividends means income focused investors cannot assume that past payouts give a clear guide to future cash returns.

data.title: Bogota Financial (BSBK) Net Interest Margin Rebound Tests Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Bogota Financial (BSBK) has just posted another profitable quarter, with Q3 FY 2025 revenue of US$4.3 million and basic EPS of US$0.04 supported by net income of US$0.5 million and a net interest margin of 1.8% as investors keep an eye on how sustainable this run rate looks. Over recent periods, the company has seen revenue move from US$3.0 million in Q2 FY 2024 to US$4.6 million in Q1 FY 2025 and US$4.0 million in Q2 FY 2025, while basic EPS shifted from a loss of US$0.03 in Q2 FY 2024 to a profit of US$0.06 in Q1 FY 2025 and US$0.02 in Q2 FY 2025. This gives a clearer line of sight on how profitability is rebuilding. With that backdrop, the latest results put the spotlight firmly on how margins are holding up as the bank balances growth with credit quality and funding costs.

1.8% net interest margin with shrinking loan book

  • Net interest margin sits at 1.8% in Q3 FY 2025, with total loans at US$671.8 million compared with US$704.1 million in Q1 FY 2025 and US$714.3 million in Q4 FY 2024.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative that focuses on high quality earnings is that the recent profitability sits alongside a smaller loan base, as net income moved from a loss of US$0.9 million in Q4 FY 2024 to a profit of US$0.7 million in Q1 FY 2025 and US$0.5 million in Q3 FY 2025. Over the same period, net interest margin moved from 1.16% on a trailing twelve month basis in Q4 FY 2024 to quarterly readings between 1.66% and 1.8% in FY 2025 so far.

    • Critics highlight that while quarterly basic EPS has swung from a loss of US$0.07 in Q4 FY 2024 to profits of US$0.06 in Q1 FY 2025 and US$0.04 in Q3 FY 2025, the cumulative five year decline suggests past profitability was materially higher than what the bank is currently generating.

data.title: Investors Should Be Encouraged By Bharat Electronics' (NSE:BEL) Returns On Capital
data.description:
To find a multi-bagger stock, what are the underlying trends we should look for in a business? One common approach is to try and find a company with returns on capital employed (ROCE) that are increasing, in conjunction with a growing amount of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at the ROCE trend of Bharat Electronics (NSE:BEL) we really liked what we saw.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on Bharat Electronics is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.32 = ₹74b ÷ (₹429b - ₹202b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to December 2025).

Thus, Bharat Electronics has an ROCE of 32%. That's a fantastic return and not only that, it outpaces the average of 17% earned by companies in a similar industry.

See our latest analysis for Bharat Electronics

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Bharat Electronics compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for Bharat Electronics .

What Can We Tell From Bharat Electronics' ROCE Trend?

Bharat Electronics is displaying some positive trends. Over the last five years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 32%. The company is effectively making more money per dollar of capital used, and it's worth noting that the amount of capital has increased too, by 95%. So we're very much inspired by what we're seeing at Bharat Electronics thanks to its ability to profitably reinvest capital.

Another thing to note, Bharat Electronics has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 47%. This effectively means that suppliers (or short-term creditors) are funding a large portion of the business, so just be aware that this can introduce some elements of risk. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

The Bottom Line On Bharat Electronics' ROCE

To sum it up, Bharat Electronics has proven it can reinvest in the business and generate higher returns on that capital employed, which is terrific. Since the stock has returned a staggering 938% to shareholders over the last five years, it looks like investors are recognizing these changes. Therefore, we think it would be worth your time to check if these trends are going to continue.

38% earnings growth but 1% five year drag

  • What stands out for a cautious, bearish view is that the recent EPS lift sits on top of quarterly swings, including a loss of ¥3.00 per share in Q2 FY 2025 and net income moving from a loss of ¥77 million in that quarter to ¥447 million in Q3 FY 2025.   • Critics with a more bearish tilt focus on this valuation gap and how it interacts with the fundamentals:
    • They highlight that a P/E above peers means the market is already pricing in relatively solid execution. Any slowdown from the recent 10% earnings growth rate could make that premium harder to justify.
    • At the same time, the trailing twelve month revenue base of ¥14,693 million and net income of ¥1,529 million show the current valuation is being applied to a business that is already quite profitable. This can limit how much further multiple expansion skeptics expect.

DCF Fair Value Sits Above ¥1,560 Share Price

  • A DCF fair value of ¥1,905.93 compared with the current share price of ¥1,560 suggests the stock is trading about 18.2% below that model based estimate.
  • What stands out for investors who lean more bullish is how this ties back to the recent earnings record:
    • The discounted cash flow output anchors on the same 10% year over year earnings growth and five year 17.8% compound rate that have supported the company’s trailing EPS of around ¥96, so the model is being fed with a history of profit expansion rather than a one off spike.
    • Because the P/E multiple is a bit above peers while the DCF fair value is above the current price, investors are effectively weighing two signals at once: a richer multiple on today’s earnings versus a model that suggests the current price sits below the cash flow based estimate.

data.title: Odfjell SE (OB:ODF) Just Reported Earnings, And Analysts Cut Their Target Price
data.description:
Odfjell SE (OB:ODF) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 7.6% to kr122 in the week after its latest yearly results. Odfjell reported in line with analyst predictions, delivering revenues of US$1.1b and statutory earnings per share of US$1.96, suggesting the business is executing well and in line with its plan. Earnings are an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance, look at what the analysts are forecasting for next year, and see if there's been a change in sentiment towards the company. So we collected the latest post-earnings statutory consensus estimates to see what could be in store for next year.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Taking into account the latest results, Odfjell's five analysts currently expect revenues in 2026 to be US$1.14b, approximately in line with the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are forecast to reduce 8.6% to US$1.79 in the same period. In the lead-up to this report, the analysts had been modelling revenues of US$1.14b and earnings per share (EPS) of US$2.17 in 2026. So there's definitely been a decline in sentiment after the latest results, noting the real cut to new EPS forecasts.

See our latest analysis for Odfjell

The average price target fell 5.4% to kr133, with reduced earnings forecasts clearly tied to a lower valuation estimate. It could also be instructive to look at the range of analyst estimates, to evaluate how different the outlier opinions are from the mean. The most optimistic Odfjell analyst has a price target of kr154 per share, while the most pessimistic values it at kr104. We would probably assign less value to the analyst forecasts in this situation, because such a wide range of estimates could imply that the future of this business is difficult to value accurately. With this in mind, we wouldn't rely too heavily the consensus price target, as it is just an average and analysts clearly have some deeply divergent views on the business.

One way to get more context on these forecasts is to look at how they compare to both past performance, and how other companies in the same industry are performing. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that Odfjell's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2026 expected to display 1.8% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 3.8% over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to see their revenue shrink 4.7% per year. So it's clear that despite the slowdown in growth, Odfjell is still expected to grow meaningfully faster than the wider industry.

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that the analysts downgraded their earnings per share estimates, showing that there has been a clear decline in sentiment following these results. On the plus side, they made no changes to their revenue estimates - and they expect it to perform better than the wider industry. Furthermore, the analysts also cut their price targets, suggesting that the latest news has led to greater pessimism about the intrinsic value of the business.

data.title: Ackermans & Van Haaren (EBR:ACKB) shareholders have earned a 18% CAGR over the last five years
data.description:
When you buy shares in a company, it's worth keeping in mind the possibility that it could fail, and you could lose your money. But on the bright side, you can make far more than 100% on a really good stock. One great example is Ackermans & Van Haaren NV (EBR:ACKB) which saw its share price drive 111% higher over five years. On top of that, the share price is up 21% in about a quarter. But this move may well have been assisted by the reasonably buoyant market (up 13% in 90 days).

So let's investigate and see if the longer term performance of the company has been in line with the underlying business' progress.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

While the efficient markets hypothesis continues to be taught by some, it has been proven that markets are over-reactive dynamic systems, and investors are not always rational. One imperfect but simple way to consider how the market perception of a company has shifted is to compare the change in the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price movement.

Over half a decade, Ackermans & Van Haaren managed to grow its earnings per share at 18% a year. This EPS growth is reasonably close to the 16% average annual increase in the share price. That suggests that the market sentiment around the company hasn't changed much over that time. Indeed, it would appear the share price is reacting to the EPS.

The image below shows how EPS has tracked over time (if you click on the image you can see greater detail).

We know that Ackermans & Van Haaren has improved its bottom line lately, but is it going to grow revenue? This free report showing analyst revenue forecasts should help you figure out if the EPS growth can be sustained.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

As well as measuring the share price return, investors should also consider the total shareholder return (TSR). The TSR incorporates the value of any spin-offs or discounted capital raisings, along with any dividends, based on the assumption that the dividends are reinvested. It's fair to say that the TSR gives a more complete picture for stocks that pay a dividend. In the case of Ackermans & Van Haaren, it has a TSR of 124% for the last 5 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. This is largely a result of its dividend payments!

A Different Perspective

We're pleased to report that Ackermans & Van Haaren shareholders have received a total shareholder return of 40% over one year. Of course, that includes the dividend. That gain is better than the annual TSR over five years, which is 18%. Therefore it seems like sentiment around the company has been positive lately. Someone with an optimistic perspective could view the recent improvement in TSR as indicating that the business itself is getting better with time. data.title: Naspers Limited's (JSE:NPN) Stock Has Shown Weakness Lately But Financial Prospects Look Decent: Is The Market Wrong?
data.description:
Naspers (JSE:NPN) has had a rough three months with its share price down 27%. But if you pay close attention, you might find that its key financial indicators look quite decent, which could mean that the stock could potentially rise in the long-term given how markets usually reward more resilient long-term fundamentals. In this article, we decided to focus on Naspers' ROE.

Return on equity or ROE is an important factor to be considered by a shareholder because it tells them how effectively their capital is being reinvested. In simpler terms, it measures the profitability of a company in relation to shareholder's equity.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Is ROE Calculated?

The formula for ROE is:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for Naspers is:

24% = US$13b ÷ US$56b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the profit over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every ZAR1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of ZAR0.24.

View our latest analysis for Naspers

What Is The Relationship Between ROE And Earnings Growth?

So far, we've learned that ROE is a measure of a company's profitability. Based on how much of its profits the company chooses to reinvest or "retain", we are then able to evaluate a company's future ability to generate profits. Assuming everything else remains unchanged, the higher the ROE and profit retention, the higher the growth rate of a company compared to companies that don't necessarily bear these characteristics.

A Side By Side comparison of Naspers' Earnings Growth And 24% ROE

At first glance, Naspers seems to have a decent ROE. Further, the company's ROE compares quite favorably to the industry average of 10%. For this reason, Naspers' five year net income decline of 13% raises the question as to why the high ROE didn't translate into earnings growth. We reckon that there could be some other factors at play here that are preventing the company's growth. For example, it could be that the company has a high payout ratio or the business has allocated capital poorly, for instance.

So, as a next step, we compared Naspers' performance against the industry and were disappointed to discover that while the company has been shrinking its earnings, the industry has been growing its earnings at a rate of 6.3% over the last few years.

Earnings growth is a huge factor in stock valuation. The investor should try to establish if the expected growth or decline in earnings, whichever the case may be, is priced in. By doing so, they will have an idea if the stock is headed into clear blue waters or if swampy waters await. Is Naspers fairly valued compared to other companies? These 3 valuation measures might help you decide.

Is Naspers Making Efficient Use Of Its Profits?

When we piece together Naspers' low three-year median payout ratio of 2.4% (where it is retaining 98% of its profits), calculated for the last three-year period, we are puzzled by the lack of growth. This typically shouldn't be the case when a company is retaining most of its earnings. So there could be some other explanations in that regard. For example, the company's business may be deteriorating.

Moreover, Naspers has been paying dividends for at least ten years or more suggesting that management must have perceived that the shareholders prefer dividends over earnings growth. Upon studying the latest analysts' consensus data, we found that the company's future payout ratio is expected to rise to 5.2% over the next three years. Consequently, the higher expected payout ratio explains the decline in the company's expected ROE (to 17%) over the same period.

Summary

In total, it does look like Naspers has some positive aspects to its business. Yet, the low earnings growth is a bit concerning, especially given that the company has a high rate of return and is reinvesting ma huge portion of its profits. By the looks of it, there could be some other factors, not necessarily in control of the business, that's preventing growth. With that said, we studied current analyst estimates and discovered that analysts expect the company's earnings growth to improve slightly. Sure enough, this could bring some relief to shareholders. To know more about the company's future earnings growth forecasts take a look at this free report on analyst forecasts for the company to find out more.
